title: String Templates
description: The elegant way to combine text and variables.

slides.title: Combining Text 🧵
slides.content:
## Mixing Strings and Variables

Often, you need to print a message that includes the live value of a variable. 

Before String Templates, you'd use the `+` operator to glue (concatenate) them together: 
`println("Hello " + name + ", your score is " + score)`

This is messy and error-prone.

slides.title: String Templates 🪄
slides.content:
In Kotlin, you can simply inject variables directly into the String using the `$` symbol!

The compiler automatically replaces `$variableName` with its real value.
slides.codeSnippet:
val name = "Alicia"
val score = 95

// So much cleaner!
println("Hello $name, your score is $score.")

quizData.question: Which special character tells Kotlin you want to inject a variable into a String?
quizData.explanation: The dollar sign `$` is used to mark a variable within a string template.

slides.title: Evaluating Expressions 🧮
slides.content:
What if you want to perform math *inside* the string? 

You can use the `${ }` syntax. Kotlin will evaluate whatever math is inside the braces and print the final result.
slides.codeSnippet:
val apples = 3
val oranges = 5

println("I have a total of ${apples + oranges} fruits.")

quizData.question: If `x = 10`, what will `println("x is $x, double is ${x * 2}")` print?
quizData.explanation: Kotlin replaces `$x` with '10' and evaluates `${x * 2}` to '20'.
